title: Permutation Subsequence

content.problemStatement:
Chefina has an array AAA consisting of NNN positive integers.

A permutation subsequence of length MMM is a subsequence that represents a permutation of length MMM.

Now, Chefina asks Chef to find the count of permutation subsequences in array AAA.
The count can be very large, so output it modulo 100000000710000000071000000007 (109+7)(10^9 + 7)(109+7).

As a reminder:


A subsequence of an array is obtained by deleting some (possibly zero) elements from the array without changing the order of the remaining elements.
A permutation of length MMM is an array of length MMM in which every element from 111 to MMM occurs exactly once.

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of multiple lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of array AAA.
The next line contains NNN space-separated integers, the elements of the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the count of permutation subsequences in array AAA, modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array A. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line containing the number of permutation subsequences of A modulo 1000000007.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first array, permutation subsequences are: [1], [2] (first 2), [2] (second 2), [3], [4], [1,2], [1,2,3]. Total 7. For the second array only [1] exists.
Only number 1 appears, so subsequences of length 1 can be chosen from any of the three positions.
Number 1 never appears, therefore no permutation subsequence exists.

solution.approach: For a fixed length M a permutation subsequence must contain each integer from 1 to M exactly once. If any number i (1 ≤ i ≤ M) does not appear in the array, such a subsequence cannot exist. Otherwise we can choose any occurrence of each i; the chosen positions are automatically distinct and after sorting by index they form a valid subsequence. Hence the number of subsequences of length M equals the product of frequencies of numbers 1…M. The answer is the sum of these products for all M for which every number 1…M is present. We compute frequencies, then iterate i = 1 upwards while freq[i] > 0, maintaining a running product (mod MOD) and adding it to the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N + maxValue) per test case, where maxValue is the largest element in the array. Over all test cases this is O(total N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(maxValue) additional memory for the frequency array.
